posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Sad. The Clape Cornas back in the 1990s was an epiphany wine for me, easily 1/3 of my “cellar” is now Northern Rhone syrah. He helped define Cornas. Brooding, powerful, gamey wines of distinction. Have not had any Cornas bottlings since a 2010 Renaissance - they need more sleep - but buy and drink the CDRs and Vin de Amis with regularity. The VDA is a perennial buy for me by the case.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Sad, indeed. He was the last of his generation since Noel Verset died three years ago.

Here’s a photo I took of him in his cellar in 1988.

(This was shot by natural light – a ~60W lightbulb – on Tri-X with a Leica M4 and a 35mm f/2 Summicron. I’m sure I shot wide open.)

posts.name: Alan Eden
posts.cooked:
Love old b & w photos

Such a depth of image

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Marcel Juge still hanging on.

John Morris:

Sad, indeed. He was the last of his generation since Noel Verset died three years ago.

Here’s a photo I took of him in his cellar in 1988.
(This was shot by natural light – a ~60W lightbulb – on Tri-X with a Leica M4 and a 35mm f/2 Summicron. I’m sure I shot wide open.)

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Fu is hustlin Juge to make one more, just
One more vintage. 2016 Fuge.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Ah, right. But when I was cutting my teeth on Northern Rhone in the 80s, Juge always took a back seat to Clape and Verset. Sorry, Charlie.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Charlie says Clape has no fruit.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr.:

Charlie says Clape has no fruit.

Just because your decades of living in humidity have made you unable to taste flavor doesn’t mean you should misquote me

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: The humid tropics are all about fruit, baby! Where do you think the exotic stuff comes from! I Need to move to a cooler climate.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Always loved John Livingstone-Learmoth’s line that if Auguste Clape were to have a monument, it should be a stone, not a statue. Seems apposite today.

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: The Clape line up is impressive work from top to bottom.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

Ah, right. But when I was cutting my teeth on Northern Rhone in the 80s, Juge always took a back seat to Clape and Verset. Sorry, Charlie.

Yup.
and lovely photo John

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
John Livingstone-Learmonth has a nice tribute to Clape on Twitter with a photo:

@DrinkRhone
Tweet 146 13 July 1925-15 July 2018 Auguste Clape, Child of Nature, my Professor and Hero. May you taste the very best Syrah above the hills of the Ardèche, mon très cher ami #clape cornas

468×680 74.6 KB

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Sad to hear this. Glad I was lucky enough to have met him two years ago. And lucky for us all that the domaine continues on in good hands.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked: RIP, Sir. (looking for the emoji where sad me is raising a glass in tribute, but this is what I found…)

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: FYI, Eric Asimov has a poignant ode to Clape on the obits page of the NY Times today.

link_counts.title: Auguste Clape, Whose Hillside Vineyards Rose to Fame, Dies at 93 - The New York Times

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked: No real words. Great wines of place and person.

posts.name: Jim Hartten
posts.cooked: I remember first buying the 1981 Clape cornas at MacArthers In DC back in the early 80s for I think $6.99 ea., and remember trying a glass and thinking “very tasty”. RIP
